title: Rescuing the Bayon: Three Decades of Discovery & Defiance
highlights: result is new masonry work that fits in visually while remaining identifiable upon close inspection to satisfy both aesthetics and conservation ethics. Interestingly, those conservation ethics connect back to the very first restorations in Angkor using the method of anastylosis developed by Henri Marchal in the early 1900s.  ...    months of work restoring deteriorated Naga balustrades, structural beams, and stone flooring using traditional anastylosis combined with modern stabilization techniques.  ...    Take the manual anastylosis methodology. This is a complete disassembly and reconstruction following the original construction sequence. Each stone gets cleaned, documented, and repaired if necessary. Then it’s repositioned with millimetre precision. In sensitive areas, the teams use hand tools exclusively without machinery that might cause mechanical damage.  Modern repair techniques

title: The Cambodian World Heritage site, Angkor Wat, is finally ...
highlights: “Nothing was done until the late 1950s, when a choice had to be made either to restore the monument completely or let it just fall apart. The Angkor Conservation Office (then run by the French) opted to act. Although invasive, such intervention was an appropriate overall response to the needs of the monument. The technical options selected were a reflection of the experience that the The Ecole Francaise d’Extreme Orient had gathered from its work on the monument dating back to the early years of the century. The “anastylosis” technique—meaning the reconstruction of a ruin using its own components—was the bottom line. We dismantled the entire structure, piece by piece, block by block and made an  ...    “There are some 300,000 pieces scattered over 10 hectares; reassembling them is somewhat like a 3D jigsaw puzzle.
